title: Power — how fast energy gets transferred
examTip: Power is measured in watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Don't confuse power (rate of energy transfer) with energy (total amount transferred).
introTips: Power is about speed of energy transfer — not how much energy in total.
A weightlifter who raises a barbell slowly does the same work as one who raises it quickly, but the faster one has more power.
Watts and joules per second are the same thing — 1 W = 1 J/s.
intro: A crane that lifts a car in 5 seconds is more powerful than one that takes 20 seconds — even though both do exactly the same job. Power tells you how quickly energy is transferred, and it's why a sports car feels different to a city car even if both eventually reach the same speed. It's also why a 2 kW kettle boils water faster than a 1 kW one, and why power stations are rated in megawatts. Understanding power lets you compare machines, calculate costs, and solve real exam questions.

keyPoints.heading: What power means
keyPoints.content: Power is the rate of energy transfer — how much energy is transferred per second. The equation is: P = E/t (power = energy ÷ time). The unit of power is the watt (W). 1 W = 1 joule per second. You can also write it as P = W/t where W is work done (in joules). A powerful machine doesn't necessarily transfer more energy overall — it transfers the same energy in a shorter time. Example: a crane lifting a car does the same work (transfers the same energy) whether it takes 5 seconds or 20 seconds. But the crane that does it in 5 seconds has four times the power: P = E/t, so for the same E, halving t doubles P.
writeBullets: Power — the rate of energy transfer; how much energy is transferred per second
P = E/t — power (W) = energy (J) ÷ time (s)
Watt (W) — the unit of power; 1 W = 1 J/s
P = W/t — power also equals work done (J) ÷ time (s)
More powerful = same energy transferred in less time, OR more energy in the same time

keyTerms.def: The rate of energy transfer; P = E/t; unit: watts (W)
The unit of power; 1 W = 1 joule per second (1 J/s)
How quickly something happens — here, how many joules transferred per second

keyPoints.memTip: P = E/t. Power is 'energy per second'. Think of it as the speed of energy transfer.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often confuse power with energy — emphasise that a 2 kW kettle and a 1 kW kettle both transfer the same energy to boil water, but the 2 kW one does it twice as fast.

keyPoints.heading: Calculating power
keyPoints.content: The main equation is P = E/t. Rearrangements: E = Pt (energy = power × time) and t = E/P (time = energy ÷ power). Worked example 1: a light bulb transfers 1800 J in 3 minutes (= 180 s) → P = 1800 ÷ 180 = 10 W. Worked example 2: a runner does 12,000 J of work in 60 s → P = 12,000 ÷ 60 = 200 W. Worked example 3: how long does a 2 kW (2000 W) heater take to transfer 36,000 J? t = E/P = 36,000 ÷ 2000 = 18 s. Unit conversions you must know: 1 kW = 1000 W. 1 MW = 1,000,000 W. Always convert to standard units (W and s) before calculating.
writeBullets: P = E/t — power (W) = energy (J) ÷ time (s)
E = Pt — energy (J) = power (W) × time (s)
t = E/P — time (s) = energy (J) ÷ power (W)
1 kW = 1000 W — always convert kilowatts to watts before calculating
1 MW = 1,000,000 W — used for large power stations and industrial equipment

keyTerms.def: Power equals energy divided by time; rearranges to E = Pt and t = E/P
1000 watts — unit used for powerful appliances and small generators
1,000,000 watts — unit used for power stations and large industrial outputs

keyPoints.memTip: Triangle trick: put P at top, E and t at bottom. Cover what you want to find: P = E/t, E = Pt, t = E/P.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often forget to convert minutes to seconds or kW to W — build in a unit check step as a habit.

keyPoints.heading: Power in everyday contexts
keyPoints.content: Putting real numbers on power helps you make sense of the world. Human body at rest: ~80 W — just keeping warm and breathing. Walking: ~300 W. Cycling: ~100–400 W depending on speed. Running: ~500–800 W (elite sprint athletes may briefly reach 2000 W). Hairdryer: ~2000 W (2 kW). Electric kettle: ~2000–3000 W. Electric car motor: 100–300 kW. Large power station: ~1–2 GW (gigawatts = 1,000,000,000 W). A professional Tour de France cyclist can sustain about 400 W for an hour — impressive, but far less than even a small electric motor. This is exactly why the Industrial Revolution was so transformative: steam engines could replace dozens of human workers by outputting many kilowatts continuously.
writeBullets: Human body at rest — ~80 W; most used for keeping warm and bodily functions
Cycling — ~100–400 W; elite cyclists sustain ~400 W for extended periods
Hairdryer / kettle — ~2 kW; high power to heat air or water quickly
Electric car motor — 100–300 kW; far more powerful than a human but very efficient
Power station — hundreds of MW to GW; gigawatts needed to power entire cities

keyTerms.def: 1,000,000,000 watts — unit used for national power output and very large power stations

keyPoints.memTip: You at rest = 80 W, a hairdryer = 2000 W, an electric car = 100,000 W. Scale matters!
keyPoints.tutor_note: Contextual power values help students with 'estimate' questions — these appear in 6-mark or multi-step problems.

keyPoints.heading: Work done and power — force × velocity
keyPoints.content: Work done (W) = force (F) × distance (d) when force and movement are in the same direction. Units: joules (J). When a force acts through a distance, energy is transferred — 'work is done'. Power can also be written as: P = Fv (power = force × velocity). This follows from P = W/t = Fd/t = F × (d/t) = Fv. Units: N × m/s = W. Worked example: a car engine exerts a driving force of 3000 N at a constant velocity of 20 m/s → P = 3000 × 20 = 60,000 W = 60 kW. This form of the equation is very useful when questions give you a force and a speed rather than energy and time. At constant velocity, the driving force equals the total resistive forces (drag + friction), so P = Fv gives you the engine power needed.
writeBullets: Work done W = Fd — force (N) × distance (m) = joules (J)
Energy is transferred when a force moves an object through a distance
P = Fv — power (W) = force (N) × velocity (m/s)
Derived from P = W/t = Fd/t = F × v
Example: 3000 N force at 20 m/s → P = 60,000 W = 60 kW

keyTerms.def: W = Fd; energy transferred when a force moves an object through a distance; unit: joule (J)
Power equals force multiplied by velocity; useful when force and speed are given instead of energy and time

keyPoints.memTip: P = Fv. Force times velocity gives power. Remember: N × m/s = W (watts).
keyPoints.tutor_note: P = Fv questions often involve a vehicle at constant speed — remind students that at constant velocity, driving force = drag, so they can find the resistive force from engine power.

tables.title: Examples of power values in context
headers: What the energy is used for
rows: Muscular work to move the body forward
Propelling bicycle against friction and air resistance
Heating air and powering a fan motor
Propelling car, overcoming drag and friction
Generating electricity for hundreds of thousands of homes
Baseload electricity generation for a whole region

1.question: A runner does 18,000 J of work in 90 seconds. What is their power output?
1.correctFeedback: Correct! P = E/t = 18,000 ÷ 90 = 200 W. A reasonable value for a runner.
1.wrongFeedback: Use P = E/t. Substitute: P = 18,000 J ÷ 90 s = 200 W. Remember to divide energy by time in seconds.

3.question: A car engine produces a driving force of 4000 N at a speed of 25 m/s. What is the engine's power output?
3.correctFeedback: Correct! P = Fv = 4000 × 25 = 100,000 W = 100 kW. A realistic value for a car engine.
3.wrongFeedback: Use P = Fv (power = force × velocity). P = 4000 N × 25 m/s = 100,000 W = 100 kW. Don't add force and velocity — multiply them.

commonMistakes: Confusing power (rate of energy transfer, W) with energy (total amount transferred, J) — they are different quantities with different units.
Forgetting to convert minutes to seconds or kW to W before calculating — always work in SI units (W and s).
Adding force and velocity instead of multiplying in P = Fv — always multiply: P = F × v.
Thinking a more powerful device always transfers more energy — it transfers energy faster, not necessarily more in total.
Leaving the unit off the answer — power must be in W (or kW/MW with conversion), not J or N.
examTips: P = E/t. If you see 'how fast' or 'rate of energy transfer', think power. If you see 'how much total energy', think E = Pt.
Convert units before calculating: kW → W (×1000), minutes → seconds (×60). Write the conversion step explicitly.
For P = Fv questions, check the units: newtons × metres per second = watts. This confirms the formula is correct.
If a question gives you efficiency and power together, find the useful output power = efficiency × input power.
revisionCardBullets: P = E/t; unit: watts (W); 1 W = 1 J/s
Work done W = Fd (force × distance); unit: joules (J)
P = Fv (power = force × velocity); useful for vehicles at constant speed
Human body at rest ~80 W; cycling ~150–400 W; hairdryer ~2 kW
Electric car motor ~100–300 kW; power station ~500 MW–2 GW
Power tells you HOW FAST energy is transferred, not how much in total
To calculate P: write formula, convert units, substitute, calculate, state units
